draft.introduction: This reconstructed core hymn draws on the Homeric Hymn to Demeter and later testimonia to present the liturgical heart of the Eleusinian Mysteries. The rites promised initiates a blessed afterlife through witnessing the sacred drama of Persephone’s return.

translation.original_ref: Reconstructed from Homeric Hymn 2 and Clement, Protrepticus II.21
translation.text: I fasted. I drank the sacred drink. I took from the chest. Having worked, I put back into the basket and from the basket into the chest. I have eaten from the drum, I have drunk from the cymbal, I have carried the sacred vessel, I have entered the bridal chamber.
translation.original_text: ἐνήστευσα, ἔπιον τὸν κυκεῶνα, ἔλαβον ἐκ κίστης, ἐργασάμενος ἀπεθέμην εἰς κάλαθον καὶ ἐκ καλάθου εἰς κίστην

translator_notes.note: This synthema or password of the Eleusinian initiate is preserved by Clement of Alexandria, who quotes it to mock the mysteries. Ironically, his hostile quotation is our best surviving evidence for the ritual formula. The exact meaning of the actions described remains debated.
